rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 30, 2019, on Line 1, PETITIONER PATRICK CORKERY Motion For New Trial Petitioner's motion for new trial is denied. Petitioner fails to demonstrate that the court's "decision is against [the] law." (Code Civ. Proc., § 657(6).) Petitioner improperly raised the argument regarding the timeliness of respondent's answer in his reply brief. As the court previously noted, petitioner had the information that it needed to assert this issue prior to filing his moving papers, but he failed to do so. The reply brief was not rebutting an argument that respondent raised. Petitioner therefore waived the matter. Petitioner's citation to and reliance on an unpublished Court of Appeal decision is improper. (Cal. R. Ct. 8.1115(a).) The court rejects Petitioner's res judicata argument, which is ill-founded. The court did not err by relying on the unsworn police report in rendering its decision. The DS 367 and the unsworn report both stated that the admonition was provided. The unsworn report could supplement the sworn DS 367. (MacDonald v. Gutierrez (2004) 32 Cal.4th 150, 159 ["the Legislature clearly anticipates the sworn report will contain all or nearly all of the information necessary to remove the offender's license. In light of this legislative intent, the sworn report cannot be wholly devoid of relevant information. However, so long as a sworn report is filed, it is consistent with the relaxed evidentiary standards of an administrative per se hearing that technical omissions of proof can be corrected by an unsworn report filed by the arresting officer."].) Respondent made a prima facie showing that the admonition was provided and petitioner provided no contrary evidence. It was therefore proper for the court to deny the petition. (See Murphey v. Shimoto (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 1052, 1070 [after department makes prima facie showing, burden shifts to driver to dispute the showing].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: DHAFIR ZIKOOR VS. RANGER PIPELINES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Morrow-Meadows Corporation Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 30 2019, on Line 4, CROSS DEFENDANT MORROW-MEADOWS CORPORATION MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Morrow-Meadows Corporation Motion For Summary Judgment Morrow-Meadows Corporation's motion for summary judgment is denied. Morrow-Meadows fails to meet its initial burden. (See Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 849 [a defendant moving for summary judgment "bears the burden of persuasion that one or more elements of the cause of action in question cannot be established, or that there is a complete defense thereto."].) Morrow-Meadows has not established there are no triable issues of fact as to whether it is liable to Plaintiff. Morrow-Meadows owed a duty of care to Plaintiff. Section IV, subdivision (i) of the contract between Morrow-Meadows and Ranger Pipelines, Inc. specifically states, "Subcontractor shall provide safe and sufficient facilities to owner, engineer, contractor, or their duly authorized representatives for inspection of the work by subcontractor." (Plaintiff's Exh. E.) Thus, Morrow-Meadows had a contractual duty to maintain a safe facility, including a duty to remove any rubbish or surplus material that may have accumulated on the job site. (See, e.g. SeaBright Ins. Co. v. US Airways, Inc. (2011) 52 Cal.4th 590, 594 [hirer implicitly delegates to the contractor any tort law duty to ensure the safety of the specific workplace that is subject of the contract].) Morrow-Meadows argues that there is no evidence to establish it committed any negligent act to breach a duty of care. However, the Court finds there is a triable issue of fact regarding whether Morrow-Meadows breached its duty of care when it failed to remove a ladder that was allegedly placed in an unsafe position, blocking a narrow walkway. (Li Decl., Ex. 5, p. 62-63; UMF 9-11.) There are numerous questions of fact to be determined at trial, such as whether the ladder was placed in a dangerous position and whether the failure to remove the ladder created an unsafe working environment for Plaintiff and others. Privette and its progeny do not apply. The Privette doctrine generally holds that when an employee of an independent contractor hired to do dangerous work suffers a work-related injury, the employee cannot recover from the person who retained or hired the independent contractor. (Privette v. Superior Court (1993) 5 Cal.4th 689, 692; Johnson v. The Raytheon Co., Inc. (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 617, 624; see also Ruiz v. Herman Weissker, Inc. (2005) 130 Cal.App.4th 52 [extending Privette rule to hirer's agent].) Here, Morrow-Meadows is not the general contractor or hirer, and Morrow-Meadows' affirmative contribution is not at issue. (See Hooker v. Department of Transportation (2002) 27 Cal.4th 198, 202 [a hirer may be liable to an employee of a contractor insofar as a hirer's exercise of retained control affirmatively contributed to the employee's injuries].) rulings.Case Title: JAMES WEST VS. PENINSULA CORRIDOR JOINT POWERS BOARD ET AL

rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 30, 2019, on Line 13, DEFENDANT PENINSULA CORRIDOR JOINT POWERS BOARD, SAN MATEO COUNTY TRANSIT DISTRICT, TRANSIT AMERICA SERVICES INC. Joint Motion To Strike Plaintiffs Second Amended Complaint Defendants' motion to strike the second amended complaint is denied. Although Plaintiff did not timely serve the second amended complaint in compliance with the court's order, Defendants did not seek entry of judgment pursuant to Code Civ. Proc., § 581(f)(2) before Plaintiff served the second amended complaint. Further, Defendants do not show that they met and conferred with Plaintiff before filing their motion to strike, as required by Code Civ. Proc., § 435.5. rulings.Case Title: JAMES WEST VS. PENINSULA CORRIDOR JOINT POWERS BOARD ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 30, 2019, on Line 13, DEFENDANT PENINSULA CORRIDOR JOINT POWERS BOARD, SAN MATEO COUNTY TRANSIT DISTRICT, TRANSIT AMERICA SERVICES INC. DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendants' demurrer to the second amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the cause of action for breach of contract. SamTrans is not a party to the Caltrain Bicycle Locker Agreement and therefore cannot be liable under that contract. Further, the contract contains an unambiguous express waiver of liability and damages provision whereby Plaintiff agreed that Caltrain and its contractor, Transmit America Services, Inc. (TASI), are not liable for any injury, loss, or damage arising from the use of any lockers. Plaintiff has not alleged sufficient facts to show that the waiver of liability provision in the contract is invalid and does not bar his contract claim against Caltrain and TASI. (See Benedek v. PLC Santa Monica, LLC (2002) 104 Cal.App.4th 1351 ["A release of all premises liability in consideration for permission to enter recreational and social facilities for any purpose does not violate public policy"] [upholding waiver of liability for personal injury in health club membership agreement]; Hulsey v. Elsinore Parachute Center (1985) 168 Cal.App.3d 333, 342 [Civ. Code § 1668 does not invalidate contracts which seek to except one from liability for simple negligence or strict liability].) For similar reasons, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend as to the cause of action for negligence against Caltrain and TASI. (See Eriksson v. Nunnink (2015) 233 Cal.App.4th 708, 722-724 [express written release extended to claims for negligence].) As previously ordered, Plaintiff may pursue his negligence claim against Samtrans. Although Plaintiff argues that he has pled sufficient facts to support a cause of action for dangerous condition of public property (Opp. at 7), the court previously sustained without leave to amend defendants' demurrer to that cause of action. The demurrer is sustained without leave to amend as to the causes of action for violations of the Bane, Ralph and Unruh Act against all three defendants. Plaintiff has not alleged sufficient facts to support those claims. (See generally Carter v. Prime Healthcare Paradise Valley LLC (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 396, 410 [where statutory remedies are invoked, facts must be pleaded with particularity].) rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Seal And Restrict Access To Records
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, December 30, 2019, on Line 8, DEFENDANT ELIZABETH PHILLIPS Ntc Of Mtn To Seal And Restrict Access To Records Grant. No opposition filed, and the motion complies with California Rules of Court, Rules 2.550 and 2.551. The Court finds that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the portions of the records to be sealed; the overriding interest supports sealing those records; a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed; the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored; and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The proposed order should repeat the required findings listed above and should list each filed document that Defendant seeks to have filed under seal, the date on which the document was filed, and the page and line numbers of the document that contain private information that Defendant seeks to have sealed. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: FRED LARSEN VS. ALISON HAHN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendant Top Dog Pet Care Llc(Dba Top Dog Sf) To Produce Its Person(S) Most Qualified And Custodian Of Records To Appear For Deposition And Production Of Documents And Request For Monetary Sanctions; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Motion; And Declaration Of Rett D.B. Bergmark, Esq.
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, December 30, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF FRED LARSEN Motion To Compel Defendant Top Dog Pet Care LLC (Dba Top Dog Sf) To Produce Its Person(S) Most Qualified And Custodian Of Records To Appear For Deposition And Production Of Documents And Request For Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Bruce Highman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The PMQ and custodian depositions that plaintiff noticed shall take place. Hearing required to set date for them. They will take place before any depositions for which defendants served notices on or after November 12, 2019. Defendants' objection based on the California Academy of Science typo is overruled. It is clear that plaintiff meant Top Dog Pet Care. Defendants' objections to Document Request Categories 2, 5-13, 17, 23 and 24 are sustained. These document requests are either not relevant at this time, or are quite overbroad and include many documents that don't appear relevant at this time. Document Request Category 25 is clarified by changing "following" to "because of." As clarified, the objection is overruled. The objections to Document Request Categories 26 and 28 are overruled. The court believes appointing a discovery referee is a good idea. But the court will not order plaintiff to pay 100 percent of the cost. Hearing required as to whether the parties are willing to pay 50 percent each. Hearing required re sanctions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to bruce.highman@highmanlaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT/BH)
